title: Ilhan Omar — U.S. Representative, Minnesota 5th District
subtitle: Profile of Congresswoman Ilhan Omar: Somali-American, first hijab-wearing member of Congress, and prominent progressive voice

content.content:
Quick Facts

Born

October 4, 1982 — Mogadishu, Somalia

District

Minnesota's 5th Congressional District

Party

Democratic–Farmer–Labor Party

In Office Since

January 3, 2019

Education

North Dakota State University (B.A.)

Ilhan Omar is a U.S. Representative serving Minnesota's 5th Congressional District since 2019. Born in Mogadishu, Somalia, she immigrated to the United States in 1995 after spending four years in a Kenyan refugee camp. She became the first Somali-American elected to Congress, the first naturalized citizen from Africa elected to the House, and one of the first Muslim women in Congress.

Life Journey

Early Life and Immigration

Born on October 4, 1982 in Mogadishu during a period of political instability, Omar's family fled Somalia during the outbreak of civil war in 1991. The family spent four years in the Dadaab refugee camp in Kenya before receiving resettlement in the United States in 1995. They settled in Minneapolis, Minnesota.

Education and Early Career

Omar later earned her Bachelor of Arts degree in political science and international studies from North Dakota State University. Before entering elected office, she worked as a community nutrition educator and was involved in grassroots organizing in the Minneapolis Somali community.

Political Career

Minnesota Legislature (2016)

In 2016, Omar was elected to the Minnesota House of Representatives, becoming the first Somali-American woman elected to a state legislature in the United States.

U.S. Congress (2018–Present)

Omar won election to the U.S. House of Representatives in 2018, defeating the Republican incumbent. She was re-elected in 2020, 2022, and 2024. She is a member of the Congressional Progressive Caucus and was a founding member of the Squad, a group of progressive Democratic congresswomen.

Committee Assignments and Policy Focus

  • House Budget Committee
  • House Education and the Workforce Committee
  • Progressive foreign policy — consistently voted against military interventions
  • Medicare for All advocate
  • Green New Deal co-sponsor
  • Immigration reform and refugee rights

Controversies and Criticism

Throughout her political career, Omar has faced significant controversies involving allegations of antisemitic remarks, campaign finance issues, and relationship disclosures. In 2023, the House Republican majority voted to remove her from the Foreign Affairs Committee.

Her critics argue her rhetoric has crossed lines into antisemitism; her supporters maintain she is engaging in legitimate criticism of Israeli government policy. The debates around her comments have sparked broader national conversations about the bounds of political speech.
